figures.caption: Table 7: Sampled MATH500 Level 4 problems used in our experiments, grouped by category. Question column gives an abbreviated statement; full problem text is recoverable from the public MATH500 release via the qid.
Figure 1: Decision ambiguity in the presence of Byzantine agents. An agent must decide which responses to trust despite adversarial and indistinguishable neighbor messages.

Table 5: Ablation over byzantine-to-weak composition at n=7 on MATH500 Level 4. The byzantine agent count (b) and weak honest count (w) are varied while the strong honest count is fixed at 4 (F=3, T=6, r=4). Strong/weak agents use gpt-4o-mini/gpt-3.5-turbo.

Table 6: Scalability to larger networks (n=9 agents) on MATH500 Level 4. Configuration: 6 strong honest, 2 weak honest, 1 adversarial Byzantine (F=3, T=6); strong/weak agents use gpt-4o-mini/gpt-3.5-turbo.
Figure 2: Venn diagram showing the partitioning of agents into reliable agents P , faulty agents F , and adversarial agents A, as well as the resulting set of honest agentsH = V \ A.

Table 8: Sampled Commonsense170k problems used in our experiments, grouped by format. Each instance preserves its native answer format; ground-truth answers are reported using the corresponding label as released. For multi-option items we list the choices in the question column.
